coredata.dc:title: Red emissive carbon dots with dual targetability for imaging polarity in living cells
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Xinxin
Hu, Yalei
Meng, Hong-Min
Yang, Jie
Qu, Lingbo
Zhang, Xiao-Bing
Li, Zhaohui
coredata.dc:description: Intracellular polarity has a large impact on the activity of a series of biomolecules or maintain the function of particular domains. Therefore, monitoring the changing of intracellular polarity can great improve the understanding its functions in living cell level. Herein, by employing p-phenylenediamine as precursor, we prepared a red emissive carbon dots based nanoprobe for real-time sensing polarity in living cells. With the increasing of the polarity, a large red shift of fluorescence emission wavelength was obtained and the fluorescence intensity of red emissive carbon dots decreased about 11-fold when the solvent changed from 10 % to 99 % water. The red emissive carbon dots possess high quantum yield, high photo-stability, good biocompatibility as well as robust dual targetability for mitochondria and lysosome. Finally, the red emissive carbon dots were successfully used to discriminate polarity differences between normal and cancer cell lines.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Red emission
Cell imaging
Fluorescence probe
Intracellular polarity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response of the R-CDs to polarity in aqueous solution

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of R-CDs. (a) TEM image, (b) DLS result, (c) FTIR spectrum and (d) XPS spectrum of R-CDs.
Optical properties of R-CDs. (a) The normalized absorption of R-CDs and (b) the normalized fluorescence spectra in different solvents.

Fluorescence response of the R-CDs to polarity in aqueous solution. (a) The fluorescence emission of R-CDs (100 μg/mL) in water–dioxane mixed solution (water from 10 to 99 %). (b) The relationship between the maximum of fluorescence emission and the proportions of water. (c) The relationship between the maximum intensity of fluorescence emission and the solvent parameter △f. (d) Photos of R-CDs in different proportions of water.
Cytotoxicity assay. The viability of HepG2 cells treated with different concentrations of R-CDs.

Co-localization assay. (a) Fluorescence imaging of HepG2 cells co-stained with R-CDs and Mito Tracker Green. (b) Fluorescence imaging of HepG2 cells stained with R-CDs and Lyso-Tracker Red. λex =488 nm, λem = 550–650 nm (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
Cell imaging assay. Confocal fluorescence imaging of HepG2 and HL-7702 cells treated with R-CDs (100 μg/mL) for 4 h. λex =488 nm, λem =. 550–650 nm.
Intracellular polarity assay. Fluorescence imaging of HepG2 cells treated with (a) DMSO + R-CDs (100 μg/mL), (b) R-CDs (100 μg/mL) and (c) HBSS + R-CDs (100 μg/mL). λex =488 nm, λem =. 550–650 nm.
The synthetic route for red emissive carbon dots and their biological application in living cells.

head.ce:title: Red emissive carbon dots with dual targetability for imaging polarity in living cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and Their Bioanalytical Applications, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and Their Bioanalytical Applications

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and Their Bioanalytical Applications, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University, Changsha 410082, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: b State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University, Changsha 410082, P. R. China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors equally contributed to this study.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Intracellular polarity has a large impact on the activity of a series of biomolecules or maintain the function of particular domains. Therefore, monitoring the changing of intracellular polarity can great improve the understanding its functions in living cell level. Herein, by employing p-phenylenediamine as precursor, we prepared a red emissive carbon dots based nanoprobe for real-time sensing polarity in living cells. With the increasing of the polarity, a large red shift of fluorescence emission wavelength was obtained and the fluorescence intensity of red emissive carbon dots decreased about 11-fold when the solvent changed from 10 % to 99 % water. The red emissive carbon dots possess high quantum yield, high photo-stability, good biocompatibility as well as robust dual targetability for mitochondria and lysosome. Finally, the red emissive carbon dots were successfully used to discriminate polarity differences between normal and cancer cell lines.

ce:section.ce:para: Polarity acts as a crucial role in chemistry and biology [1]. A variety of organic and inorganic reaction processes are mainly dependent on the surrounding polarity [2]. In living system, the polarity can reflect a number of sophisticated physiological functions and pathological effects. For example, polarity charges protein binding, signal transduction, membrane rearrangement and immune response activation. Furthermore, it has been proved that many disorders and diseases are resulted from the abnormal level of polarity [3,4]. Hence, monitoring the changing of intracellular polarity is important for understanding its biological functions and diagnosis of disease. However, the polarity detection is very difficult because it contains many noncovalent interactions, such as hydrogen bonding, dipolarity and polarizability [5,6]. Hence, it is highly desired to develop the efficient strategies to detect the polarity level in living cells.

Fluorescent imaging technique has unique advantages on monitoring intracellular species due to its high sensitivity, real-time imaging, and minimal damaging effects [7–10]. Consequently, an increasing number of polarity-sensitive organic small molecular based fluorescent sensors have been reported for sensing of polarity [11,12]. Nevertheless, some inherent shortcomings hampered their applications in intracellular environment, such as low quantum yield, poor water solubility, easy photo-bleaching, high biotoxicity and complicated preparation procedures. Hence, it is still highly needed to develop a fluorescent probe with easy to preparation, high self-delivery efficiency and stability in living cells, as well as good biocompatibility.

As a carbon-based nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have received increasing attention in the recent years because of their high photo-stability, low-cost, and excellent biocompatibility [13]. Many studies have shown the applications of CDs in various fields including biosensing [14–17], biolabeling and medical imaging [18–22], and cancer therapy [23,24]. And among them, the R-CDs have attracted increasing attention because they have deep tissue penetration ability, little damage to detection of samples and minimum auto-fluorescence background of bio-samples [25].

In addition, several environment-sensitive CDs have been reported with emission color changing in different solvents. Pan et al. explained on preparation of polarity-sensitive CDs using pyrolysis process. The results have shown that under the same excitation wavelength, the emission wavelength of the CDs varied from 400 to 430 nm with the change of polarity [26]. Lin and co-workers have used m-phenylenediamine as precursor to synthesize the CDs having polarity-dependent emission from 400 to 500 nm [27]. Wang et al. also reported a method to prepare the CDs, which successfully responded to the change of polarity and the luminescence color of CDs had an obvious change [28]. Though they are useful for sensing polarity, however, these CDs based probes only were used for sensing polarity in buffer solution. Based on these previous studies, we try to design a red emission CDs based nanoprobe to sense the polarity in living system.

In this work, by using p-phenylenediamine as precursor, we prepared polarity-sensitive, red color-emitting CDs (R-CDs) through a hydrothermal method (Scheme 1 ). The photoluminescence color of the R-CDs is varied from 525 to 612 nm in different solvent with the increasing of polarity. The proposed R-CDs show a linear response range from 10 % to 99 % water. Moreover, these R-CDs possess dual targetability due to the abundant amino, pyridine, and delocalized positive cation lipophilic cationic moieties on the surface of R-CDs. Finally, the R-CDs was successfully utilized to detect the change of polarity in different cell lines through imaging the intracellular polarity.

p-Phenylenediamine was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Co., Ltd. (St. Louis, MO.USA). The organic solvents of toluene, chloroform, ethyl acetate, dichloromethane, dimethyl sulphoxide (DMSO), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), acetone, ethanol, acetonitrile, and methanol were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). CellTiter 96 Aqueous One Solution Cell Proliferation Assay (MTS) was obtained from Promega. All chemicals were used directly without further purification.

The R-CDs were obtained through a hydrothermal method. Briefly, 0.8 g of p-phenylenediamine was first dissolved in 80 mL of ethanol, and then the solution was sonicated for 15 min to get a well-distributed solution. After that, the solution was placed into a poly (tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclaves and heated at 200 °C. After 12 h, the solution was cooled down to 25 °C. The obtained dark-red solution was centrifuged at the speed of 6577×g for 15 min, and then the precipitate was collected. A silica column chromatography was used to purify the collected crude products and the mixture of ethanol and ethyl acetate was used as eluents. Finally, the solvents in the products were removed and then the products were dried under a vacuum to obtain the final R-CDs.

TEM analyses were carried out on a transmission electron microscope (FEI-Tecnai G2, USA) with a working voltage of 200 kV. Hydrodynamic size was obtained on a Nano Plus-3 particle size and zeta potential analyzer (Micromeritics, USA). The surface functional groups of R-CDs were examined using Fourier-transform infrared (NEXUS-470 spectrometer, Nicolet, USA) with KBr pellets. An EscaLab 250Xi photoelectron spectrometer (USA) was used to analyse the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) experiment. Ultraviolet-visible absorption data were measured on an UV–vis spectrophotometer (Agilent 8453, USA) with a 1 cm quartz cell. Fluorescence spectrum was recorded on a F-7000 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) with a 400 μL quartz cuvette. The absolute quantum yield and lifetime of R-CDs were measured on a FLS 980 fluorometer (Edinburgh Instrument, British). The MTS assay was operated on the Spark™ Multimode Microplate Reader (Tecan, Switzerland). Confocal fluorescence imaging experiments were carried out on a DMi8A laser scanning confocal microscope (Leica, Germany).

To study the cytotoxicity of R-CDs to living cells, a standard MTS assay was carried out on HepG2 cells. 4 × 103 HepG2 cells per well were first seeded in a 96-well plate and incubated in an incubator for 24 h. Then, the old medium was discarded and the cells were washed three times with fresh RPMI 1640 medium. Followed by this step, 200 μL of fresh medium with different concentrations of R-CDs (0∼400 μg/mL) was added to each test well and incubated for another 48 h at 37 °C. After that, 100 μL of 6 times diluted MTS solution was added to all wells and incubated for 1 h under the same condition. Finally, the absorbance value of each well at 490 nm was recorded by the Spark™ Multimode Microplate Reader.

The ability of our proposed R-CDs for monitoring intracellular polarity level in living cells was evaluated by confocal imaging experiment. Different kind of cell lines were seeded in 20 mm glass-bottom dishes and cultured for overnight at 37 °C using RPMI 1640 culture medium with 10 % (v: v) fetal bovine serum and 0.5 % (v: v) penicillin and streptomycin. Subsequently, all cells were washed several times using PBS solution and then treated with 100 μg/mL. After 4 h, cells were incubated with corresponding organelle-specific dyes for 15 min. Cells were washed several times with 800 μL of PBS and then put on confocal fluorescence microscopy to image.

In our system, R-CDs was prepared through a hydrothermal method by employing p-phenylenediamine as single precursor. The diameter size and morphology of the prepared R-CDs were investigated by the TEM technique. From the results we can see that the diameters of R-CDs are about 5 nm and are nearly spherical with good monodispersity (Fig. 1 a). The size distribution of R-CDs was characterized by dynamic light scattering (DLS). DLS result (Fig. 1b) shows that the diameter for R-CDs is between 2 nm and 8 nm.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) was used to study the functional groups on the surface of R-CDs. From Fig. 1c, the characteristic stretching vibration of OH, C–H and CN are observed at 3408 cm−1, 2930 cm−1 and 1638 cm−1, respectively. The absorption peak at 1493 cm−1, 1304 cm−1 and 1246 cm−1 correspond to CN and the stretching peak at 1111 cm−1 is assigned to COC. These results indicate that there are abundant of -NH2 on the surface of R-CDs, which is very beneficial for acidic lysosome targeting [29].

This study has shown that R-CDs not only exhibited outstanding solubility in a series of organic solvents but also possess a clear solution in water when pre-treated with ultrasound. We first investigated their absorption and emission spectra properties in different solvents covering a large polarity range (according to Lippert's polarity scale, △f). As shown in Fig. 2 a, as the increasing of solvent polarity, the absorption wavelength of R-CDs shows slightly changes. However, the polarity of solvent has an obvious influence on the emission wavelength. With the increasing of the solvent polarity, the emission spectra display a significant red-shift from 525 to 612 nm with the same excitation of 420 nm (Fig. 2b). Such phenomena may be due to the changed surface states of R-CDs in different environment.

The quantum yield and fluorescence lifetime of the R-CDs in series of solvents were also investigated and the results were recorded in Table S1. As shown in Table S1, R-CDs exhibited the highest quantum yield of 47.87 % in DMF with a yellow color and a lower quantum yield of 3.40 % in water with a red color. In addition, with the polarity △f changed from 0.013 to 0.321, the values of quantum yield and lifetime show linearity at some extent, which are agreed with the emission wavelength. These all results suggested that our proposed R-CDs can be used to monitor the change of the polarity.

Before the R-CDs was used to sensing the polarity in living systems, the effect of pH on the fluorescence property of R-CDs was first investigated. Due to the low solubility and quantum yield of R-CDs in water, series of mixed solutions with different pH were used to study the effect of pH on the fluorescence property of R-CDs. These mixed solutions consisted of DMSO and buffer solution (1:1 v/v). As shown in Fig. S3, the intensity of R-CDs exhibits little change from pH 5 to pH 12, suggesting that the intracellular pH will not influence the fluorescence emission of R-CDs.

Moreover, we also investigated the photostability of R-CDs in DMSO under a continuous irradiation (150 W, Xe discharge lamp). Comparing with Rhodamine 6 G, our prepared R-CDs show higher stability under the same experimental condition. As shown in Fig. S4, there is only about 6.0 % of decrease for R-CDs after 60 min of irradiation, but more than 26.7 % of decrease for Rhodamine 6 G is observed. Then the photostability of R-CDs in HepG-2 cells was also investigated. As shown in Fig. S5, the R-CDs treated cells show slightly fluorescence change with continuous irradiation with 488 nm laser, while Rhodamine 6 G treated cells show obviously fluorescence decrease as the irradiation time increasing from 0 to 35 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of the R-CDs to polarity in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: Before the R-CDs was used to monitor the polarity in living systems, the fluorescence response of R-CDs to polarity in solutions was examined. Solutions with different polarity were obtained by mixing water and dioxane according to different volume ratios and the proportion of water changed from 10 % to 99 % to represent the increasing of polarity. When the proportion of water increased from 10 % to 99 %, the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs decreased about 11-fold (Fig. 3 a). Moreover, the emission maximum wavelength has an obvious red-shifted. Fig. 3b and Fig. 3c describe the relationship between the fluorescence intensity maximum and proportions of water and △f of the solution, respectively. Both of them can obtain good linear correlation (R2 = 0.9722 or 0.9985). However, the absorbance of R-CDs in mixtures of water from 10 to 99 % water only has a little red-shift (Fig. S6). All the results indicate that our prepared R-CDs exhibits extremely sensitive to polarity change. Another advantageous for R-CDs is the obvious color change of the fluorescence emission with the change of solvent polarity. As shown in Fig. 3d, the color change from a reddish prominent orange in 10 % water to a dark red in 90 % water. The high photostability, high sensitivity and obvious color change make the R-CDs easy for detecting the slight change of polarity in living system.

When designing a nanoprobe to be used in living systems, the cytotoxicity of the nanosystem is an important factor to be considered. The cytotoxicity of R-CDs was investigated by the Aqueous One Solution Cell Proliferation Assay (MTS). The result showed that the R-CDs exhibits negligible cytotoxicity towards HepG2 cells (liver cancer) even the concentration of R-CDs up to 400 μg/mL, indicating R-CDs possess the good biocompatibility and can be used for cellular imaging experiments (Fig. 4 ).

Before the co-localization assay in living cell, the interaction between corresponding commercial organelle-specific dyes (Mito-Tracker Red and Lyso-Tracker Red) and R-CDs was first investigated. As shown in Fig. S7, there is no obvious interaction between commercially available organelle specific dye and R-CDs. After which, co-localization study of R-CDs was investigated in HepG2 cells, and the result show that the fluorescence image of R-CDs partially overlaps with the region stained with Mito-Tracker Red (Fig. 5 a), and the other parts overlaps well with Lyso-Tracker Red stained area (Fig. 5b). These result suggest that the R-CDs can preferentially accumulate both in lysosomes (Pearson correlation coefficient, ρ = 88.4 %) and mitochondria (Pearson correlation coefficient, ρ = 72.3 %).

The obvious responses to the polarity and good biocom [1–31] patibility as well as unique dual targeting property are favorable for the prepared R-CDs to image polarity in living cells. To assess the feasibility of the prepared R-CDs in a biological environment, confocal fluorescence imaging experiments were carried out to detect intracellular polarity in cancer and normal cell lines. In this study, both HepG2 cells and HL-7702 cells (normal human liver cells) were treated with R-CDs. As illustrated in Fig. 6 a, a strong red fluorescence is observed in HepG2 cells, and only very weak fluorescence can be seen in HL-7702 (Fig. 6b). Similar phenomenon was also observed in Hela cells and mouse embryonic fibroblast 3T3 cells (Fig. S8). All these results demonstrated that polarity in normal cells is higher than that in cancer cells, and strongly support the feasibility of intracellular polarity monitoring by using R-CDs.

Lastly, the R-CDs were further utilized to monitor the change of polarity in living cells. HepG2 cells were pre-treated with 100 μg/mL of R-CDs, and then 10 μL of DMSO was added to cell culture medium. DMSO can be quickly absorbed by living cells and decrease the intracellular polarity. In another control group, HepG2 cells were first incubated in the Hank’s Balanced Salt Solution (HBSS), which is a medium without nutrient and can increase the intracellular polarity, and then treated with 100 μg/mL of R-CDs. As shown in Fig. 7 , compared with cells without pre-treatment, an obvious fluorescence enhancement has been found in the cells after treatment with DMSO. However, only very weak fluorescence can be observed in the cells after incubated in HBSS for 24 h. These results suggest that these R-CDs can be used to sense the change of the polarity in living systems.

In summary, we have developed a polarity-sensitive red emissive CDs with a high fluorescence quantum yield, high photostability and excellent biocompatibility. In this study, the R-CDs exhibited high sensitivity to polarity and was also successfully applied to discriminate the cancer cells and normal cells. Moreover, this R-CDs possesses dual targetability both to mitochondria and lysosome. We believe that this R-CDs based nanoprobe may pave the research on the mitochondrial and lysosomal polarity-related studies.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant number 21605038 and 21877027), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (grant numbers 2016M602245 and 2019T120623), the Key Scientific Research Project in Universities of Henan Province (grant number19A150048), the Foundation for University Key Teacher by Henan Province (grant numbers 2017GGJS007).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2019.127582.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix B
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrasensitive fluorescent probe for the hydrophobic range of solvent polarities

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: X. W. Hua, Y. W. Bao, Z. Chen, F. G. Wu, Nanoscale, 9(2017) 10948-10960. 10.1039/C7NR03658B.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xinxin Shi received her Bachelor degree from the College of Chemistry at Zhengzhou University in 2017. She has been studying for her Ph.D. degree in the College of Chemistry at Zhengzhou University since 2018, where her main research interests are novel functional nucleic acid-conjugated nanostructures for bioassays, bioimaging and drug delivery.

Yalei Hu received her Master degree from the College of Chemistry at Zhengzhou University in 2018. He has been studying for his Ph.D. degree in the College of Chemistry at Freie Universität Berlin since 2018. He current research interests focuses on the preparing of carbon dots and their applications.

Hong-Min Meng graduated with her B.S. in 2010 and PhD in 2015, both from the Department of Chemistry, Hunan University. Currently, she has been an assistant professor in the Department of Chemistry at Zhengzhou University. Her research interests include constructing novel functional nucleic acid-conjugated nanostructures for bioassay, bioimaging and drug delivery.

Jie Yang received her Master degree from the College of Chemistry at Zhengzhou University in 2019. She has been studying for her Ph.D. degree in the College of Chemistry at Zhengzhou University since 2019. Her current research interests focuses on the preparing of carbon dots and their applications.

Lingbo Qu received his PhD degree in Pharmaceutical Analysis at China Pharmaceutical University in 1997. He did his postdoctoral training at the Pharmaceutical Institute at Peking Union Medical College from 1997 to 1999. At present, he is a full Professor at Zhengzhou University. His research interests are mainly focused on drug molecule design and drug analysis.

Xiao-Bing Zhang is a professor in the Department of Chemistry at Hunan University. He completed his B.S. in 1993 and Ph.D. in 2001, both in chemistry from Hunan University. He worked at the Ecole Normale Superieure de Lyon (France) and the Royal Institute of Technology (Sweden) as a postdoctoral fellow from 2003 to 2005. He served as an invited professor at ENS de Lyon in 2008 and as a visiting professor at the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign in 2009. Professor Zhang’s research interests concern fluorescent chemosensors and functional DNA-based biosensors.

Zhaohui Li received his Ph.D. degree from State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics at Hunan University in 2007. He did his postdoctoral training in Walt Group at Tufts University from 2007 to 2009 and in Pacific Northwest National Laboratory from 2009 to 2011. At present, he is a full professor at Zhengzhou University. His research interests mainly focus on nanomaterial preparation and their applications in Chemo/Biosensing.
